title: Life expectancy, including UN projections
subtitle: The [period life expectancy](#dod:period-life-expectancy) at birth. This includes the observed life expectancy since 1950, and the medium-variant projections for the future, based on estimates by the UN Population Division.
caption:
Life expectancy, including UN projections

The period life expectancy at birth. This includes the observed life expectancy since 1950, and the medium-variant projections for the future, based on estimates by the UN Population Division.

The line chart tracks period life expectancy at birth from 1950 through 2100, measuring years on the vertical axis against time on the horizontal axis. Solid lines represent observed historical data from 1950 to roughly 2023, while dotted lines extend the trajectory into the future based on UN medium-variant projections. Japan and Sweden occupy the highest positions throughout the timeline, starting near 70 years in 1950 and rising steadily to approximately 85 years by 2020, with projections placing them between 90 and 95 years by 2100. Brazil and India begin at significantly lower levels, with India starting near 40 years and Brazil near 50 years in 1950. Both nations show steep upward trends in the solid-line era, reaching the 70-year mark around 2020. The World average line sits between these groups, climbing from roughly 48 years in 1950 to over 70 years in the present day. A distinct dip appears in the India and World lines around 2020 before the projected recovery begins. By 2100, the dotted lines show a convergence pattern where the gap between the highest and lowest values narrows compared to 1950, though Japan remains at the top of the range and India remains at the bottom. The visual structure displays a consistent upward slope for all entities across the entire century, with the rate of increase appearing steeper for the lower-starting nations during the observed period.
